  • Simulate the dynamic values a Date Function will return, before it goes live
  • Transport Date Functions from development through to quality and production systems

This is a no-code capability — no developer involvement needed to define or adjust Date Functions. It puts time-intelligence directly in the hands of functional consultants and power users.

 

2. Developer — Use in CDS Views

The Developer persona integrates Date Functions directly into CDS view source code.

A developer can declare a Date Function parameter in a CDS view with a default value. This makes the CDS view inherently time-aware — the date range it operates on is no longer hardcoded but driven by a named, maintainable definition.

Key users can then further customise Date Function parameters through apps like Custom Analytical Queries, without touching the underlying code.

 

Coming soon: SAP is also working on enabling developers to define Date Functions directly within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) — using the same development environment as CDS views, with a form-based UI and simulate/test capabilities built in. This is currently in progress (WIP) and subject to change, but it will further unify the developer experience.

3. Business User — Consume in Analytical Reports

The Business User benefits from Date Functions through Analytical Reporting tools built on Date-Function-enabled CDS views.

Instead of entering raw date values, users:

  • Select from a value help showing all available Date Functions with their human-readable names
  • Easily toggle between different Date Functions to shift the analysis window — for example, switching from CURRENT_MONTH to PREVIOUS_MONTH with a single selection
  • Get results that are always current and accurate, because the date range resolves at query execution time

This dramatically simplifies the reporting experience and reduces the chance of user error when specifying time periods.

 

4. ABAP Developer — Consume via API

For developers who need to use dynamic date logic in ABAP programs, reports, or classes, Date Functions are accessible via a C1-released CDS view API.

This API returns the resolved dynamic date or date range for a given Date Function — which the developer can then use directly in SELECT statements or any time-based processing logic.

 
 
" Example: Use the Date Function API to resolve a dynamic date range
" and filter records within that time frame
SELECT * FROM your_cds_view
  WHERE posting_date BETWEEN @LV_start_date AND @LV_end_date.

The value help integration also works in ABAP contexts, making it easy to discover and reference available Date Functions programmatically.

 

Coming soon: SAP is also working on enabling developers to define Date Functions directly within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) — using the same development environment as CDS views, with a form-based UI and simulate/test capabilities built in. This is currently in progress (WIP) and subject to change, but it will further unify the developer experience.

Why This Matters for Steampunk / BTP ABAP Cloud Environment

If you're building on the SAP BTP ABAP Environment (Steampunk), Date Functions are particularly valuable because:

  • They align with the clean core philosophy — time logic lives in a maintainable, transportable definition rather than scattered across code
  • They support extensibility without modification — key users can evolve date definitions independently of developer cycles
  • The CDS parameter approach keeps analytical views flexible and reusable across multiple time horizons
  • The upcoming ADT integration will make Date Functions a first-class citizen of the developer toolchain, consistent with how other CDS artefacts are managed

Summary

Persona Tool Capability
Key UserManage Date Functions appDefine, simulate, and transport Date Functions
DeveloperCDS ViewsDeclare Date Function parameters with defaults
Business UserAnalytical ReportsSelect Date Functions via value help at runtime
ABAP DeveloperABAP Development Tool Kit (ADT)Resolve dynamic dates for use in ABAP logic (WIP)

 

Coming soon: SAP is also working on enabling developers to define Date Functions directly within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) — using the same development environment as CDS views, with a form-based UI and simulate/test capabilities built in. This is currently in progress (WIP) and subject to change, but it will further unify the developer experience.

ABAP Developer Define and Consume Custom Date Function in ADT (Development User)

High-Impact Use Cases

  • Dynamic Financial Period Reporting: Instead of hard-coding dates, developers can use the PREVIOUSMONTH or Fiscal Year conversion reuse functions. This allows an analytical dashboard to automatically adjust its data range based on the system's current date without any code changes.
  • SLA and Deadline Tracking: Partners can leverage standardized functions like DATN_DAYS_BETWEEN or utcl_add_seconds to calculate service level agreement (SLA) breaches or invoice due dates in real-time. This ensures that "Time to Resolve" calculations are identical whether they run on-stack in S/4HANA or side-by-side on BTP.
  • Time-Zone Aware Global Operations: For apps serving users in different regions, reuse components like abap_system_timezone or utcl_to_local automatically handle daylight savings and regional conversions. This is critical for logistics or workforce management apps where a "Requested Delivery Date" must be accurately localized for warehouses worldwide.

Key Benefits of Adoption

  • Zero-Downtime Upgrades: Because these components are "Released APIs" (C1 contract), they are guaranteed to remain stable during automated SAP cloud upgrades.
  • Performance Optimization: Date Functions is significantly faster than fetching data into the application layer for manual date math.
  • Reduced Development Maintenance: By using Date Functions partners offload the maintenance.

 

 👉 Please let us know if you have any busines case / functional usecase in which you would like to use the Custom Date Function in ABAP Development Tool Kit (ADT) 

👉 Drop your experience or questions in the comments — always happy to discuss!

👉reach out to murthy.v@sap.com achim.clemens@sap.com nitin.padmanabhan@sap.com ashwin.k.s@sap.com 

👉Have you used Date Functions in your projects? Drop your experience or questions in the comments — always happy to discuss!

entry.title: The Patient SAP Technical Architect
entry.content:
I have spent years writing things no human will ever read. Behavior definitions. CDS annotations. Authorization objects with names like F_BKPF_BUK. My work lived in layers below the surface of the enterprise — invisible, load-bearing, essential. The user clicked a button. I was the reason it worked. We never met.

SAP Joule is the first thing in thirty years of SAP's history that changes that relationship. Not for the user — they always had the button. It changes it for me, the architect. Joule is, in some sense, a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote.

I. What It Actually Is

Let us be precise before we are poetic.

Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot, available since late 2023 across S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Analytics Cloud, and BTP. It sits as a panel on the right side of your Fiori Launchpad. You type a question in plain language. It answers using your live SAP data — not a simulation, not a demonstration dataset, not a hallucination dressed in corporate clothing.

The architecture underneath is worth understanding. There are large language models — partnerships with Mistral, Anthropic, and others — running inside SAP AI Core on BTP. But the models alone are not what makes Joule interesting. What makes it interesting is the grounding layer: a proprietary business context engine that tethers every LLM response to real SAP master data, transactional records, and configuration. The authorization layer then filters that response through your role's existing permission objects.

Joule will not invent a vendor name. It will not fabricate an invoice amount. It reads what is actually in your system, through the authorizations you actually hold. This is the thing that separates it from every consumer AI product your CFO has already tried to ban.

Retrieval Augmented Generation fetches SAP Help documentation and live business data at query time. The result is a response grounded in both SAP's knowledge and your company's reality simultaneously. For an architect who has spent years building that reality — one CDS view at a time — this is not a small thing.

II. What Changes for the Developer

In Business Application Studio, Joule is embedded as an intelligent coding assistant that understands not just syntax, but intent within SAP's specific frameworks. This is the distinction that matters.

A general coding assistant knows JavaScript. Joule knows CAP. It knows that a @readonly annotation in a projection entity has specific implications for the generated OData service. It knows RAP — managed and unmanaged scenarios, the difference between a BDEF with managed implementation and one with a handler class. It knows Fiori annotations well enough to suggest @ui.lineItem positioning without being told the rendering context.

Consider what used to take an afternoon:

-- A developer once spent 3-4 hours writing:
-- Entity definition, associations, access controls,
-- projection view, service definition, BDEF,
-- behavior implementation stubs.

-- Now the prompt is:
"Generate a managed RAP Business Object for Equipment
Maintenance. Header has Equipment, Description, PlantCode,
Category. Items have Task, PlannedDate, Status. Include
mandatory field validations and a confirmMaintenance action."

-- Joule produces all of it. In seconds.
Time savings documented in SAP's own benchmarks:
CDS view generation 2–4 hours → 15 minutes.
RAP BDEF generation 3–5 hours → 30 minutes.
Unit test generation 2–3 hours → 20 minutes.

The productivity numbers are real. But productivity is the least interesting thing happening here.

What is actually happening is a shift in what the developer's day consists of. If Joule generates the scaffolding, I am no longer spending my cognitive energy on the ceremony of SAP development — the syntax rituals, the framework boilerplate, the documentation archaeology. I am spending it on the decisions that actually require judgment: data modeling choices, authorization concept design, integration topology, performance trade-offs that the LLM cannot make without business context only I possess.

The parts of the job that were tedious are becoming automated. The parts that required taste are becoming more visible.

III. What Changes for the Architect

The technical architect's role has always been translation. Business says: we need to track equipment maintenance across three plants with approval workflows. The architect translates that into a set of data structures, authorization concepts, integration points, and deployment decisions. Then the architect communicates that translation downward to developers and upward to stakeholders.

Joule compresses the translation loop.

An architect can now ask Joule to explain the current purchasing organization structure of a client system in plain language — not to learn it themselves, but to generate documentation that previously would have taken a day to write. An architect can ask Joule to review a CDS view for performance issues and receive back a structured analysis with specific optimization suggestions. An architect can use Joule in Joule Studio to build custom AI skills that extend Joule's capabilities to the client's specific domain — fleet management, specialized procurement categories, industry-specific compliance — without writing LLM infrastructure from scratch.

The architect who understands Joule's grounding architecture — RAG over SAP content, live data APIs, role-based authorization filtering — can design systems that work with the AI layer rather than around it. This is the new literacy.

Joule Studio is worth dwelling on. It is SAP's low-code environment for building custom AI skills — defining intents, entities, actions, and knowledge bases that extend Joule to domains SAP itself has not anticipated. An architect who can define a skill scope, enumerate the 15 most common user intents, connect them to CAP service endpoints, and upload a policy knowledge base for RAG-based answering is delivering something qualitatively different from the architects who cannot.

The custom skill architecture is clean in its separation of concerns: user request → intent recognition → skill routing → backend API call → structured result → natural language response. Every piece of this maps onto patterns architects already know. The new piece is the intent layer. Learning to design for intent rather than for transaction is the shift.

IV. On Security and the Things Clients Ask

Every architect deploying Joule will face the same questions in the same order. They deserve direct answers.

Does our data go to OpenAI? No. Joule runs LLMs inside SAP AI Core — SAP's tenanted infrastructure on BTP. Your data does not reach public AI providers. The BTP data center region you select determines data residency.

Can users see what they shouldn't? No. Joule inherits your existing SAP authorization objects. The same XSUAA integration and IAM role collections that govern Fiori app access govern what Joule can retrieve and display. An AP clerk asking Joule about payroll data receives the same result as that clerk opening the payroll app directly: nothing.

Does Joule learn from our data and share it? No. Tenant isolation is strict. Your interaction data does not train shared models.

Can Joule take destructive actions? Only within the user's authorization scope, and with explicit confirmation for significant actions. Joule does not bypass workflow approvals. It can initiate them.

The governance framework an architect should recommend to clients has five components: an AI usage policy defining acceptable prompts, a data classification guide for what belongs in prompt text, audit logging via SAP Audit Log Service, access control via BTP Role Collections, and a custom skill review process before any new skills reach production.

This is not exotic. It is the same governance discipline good architects have always applied to system access. The vocabulary is new. The principles are familiar.

V. The Prompting Problem

There is a skill that SAP calls the CRAFT framework — Context, Role, Action, Format, Target — for constructing effective prompts. Architects deploying Joule will find themselves teaching this to end users. They will also need to build what the guide calls a Prompt Library: a collection of validated, role-specific prompts that become a deliverable of the implementation project itself.

This is not trivial. A prompt is a specification. A bad specification produces bad output. The work of prompt engineering is, at its core, the same work as requirements engineering: making implicit intent explicit enough that a system can act on it reliably.

-- Vague (produces vague output):
"Show me sales data"

-- Precise (produces precise output):
"As the Regional Sales Manager, summarize in a table
the top 10 customers by overdue receivables for
company code 1000 as of today, showing amount,
days overdue, and responsible account manager."

Architects who understand why good prompts work — because they reduce the search space of possible interpretations — will build better prompt libraries. They will also build better training programs, because they can explain the underlying principle rather than just distributing a list of magic phrases.

VI. What the Roadmap Actually Means

SAP has stated its intention to make Joule the primary interface for all SAP applications by 2026. The roadmap includes agentic AI — Joule executing multi-step business processes autonomously — and cross-application orchestration: one prompt triggering actions in S/4HANA, Ariba, and SAC simultaneously without human mediation at each step.

Architects should read this carefully.

Agentic AI changes the error model. When a human executes a sequence of SAP transactions, each step is a checkpoint. When an agent executes them, the checkpoints are only where you designed them. The architect who builds agentic workflows must think carefully about where confirmation steps live, what constitutes a reversible versus irreversible action, and how audit trails are maintained across a sequence initiated by a single natural language instruction.

This is not a reason to be afraid of agentic AI. It is a reason for architects to be involved in its design rather than observing from outside. The governance questions — what can an agent do without human confirmation, what triggers a human-in-the-loop, how do we reconcile agent actions with existing workflow approval hierarchies — are architectural questions. They require people who understand both the business process and the technical substrate.

The architects who will matter most in the next five years are not the ones who resist AI augmentation. They are the ones who understand it precisely enough to design its guardrails.

VII. A Note on What Disappears

When a senior user of SAP needs to find the payment status of a vendor invoice, they navigate to FBL1N, enter the vendor number, set the selection criteria, execute the report, read the results. This takes two minutes if you know what you are doing. It takes fifteen minutes if you do not, and it takes a phone call to IT if you have forgotten the T-code entirely.

Joule collapses this to: "What is the payment status for vendor Bosch?"

The T-code does not disappear. The CDS view behind the Fiori app that FBL1N has become does not disappear. The authorization object F_LFA1_BUK that controls whether this user can see this vendor's data does not disappear. Everything I built is still there. What disappears is the requirement that the user know any of it.

This is the correct outcome. Users should never have needed to know T-codes. That was always an implementation detail leaking into the user experience — a failure of interface design that we normalized because we had no better option. Joule is the better option, thirty years late.

For the developer, this means the contract changes. I am no longer primarily responsible for navigation design or information architecture in the traditional sense. I am responsible for the quality of the data models and service definitions that Joule reads. If my CDS associations are poorly structured, Joule's answers will be poorly structured. If my authorization concepts are coarse-grained, Joule's data filtering will be coarse-grained. The user never sees my code. They see Joule's response to my code. The distance between my work and its consequence has compressed.

I began by saying that Joule is a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote. That is not quite right. More precisely: Joule is the interface through which the things I built finally learned to speak for themselves.

The patient labor of enterprise software architecture — the years of CDS views and RAP objects and authorization concepts — was always in service of business processes that required this complexity to function. The complexity was never the goal. The goal was always the vendor getting paid, the employee going on leave, the purchase order reaching the right supplier on time.

Joule does not make the complexity disappear. It makes the complexity invisible, which is what the complexity always wanted to be. My job, as architect, is to ensure the invisible infrastructure is worthy of the interface that now speaks for it.

That is not a smaller job. It is, finally, the right one.

entry.title: RAP Recommendations
entry.content:
Sometimes, filling out forms is quite cumbersome. Like when you need to select the country you live in from a dropdown and scroll through all the countries, while you know that the application could have just preselected the country. Going further, would it not be great to write a description of where you are traveling and have recommendations for the city automatically generated, such as accommodations or restaurants?

To achieve this, RAP recommendations are available with SAP BTP ABAP environment 2602 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2602. RAP recommendations are an input assistance that proposes field values for input fields in a RAP application on an SAP Fiori UI. The proposed field values are based on the user input and the interaction history.

Motivation

Before the introduction of RAP recommendations, business users had to manually enter input into input fields or select values from a value help. The problem with that is:

  • Data entries may differ slightly from user to user.
  • Thinking about each entry is not efficient.
  • Finding the right terms is challenging.

By providing RAP recommendations, these difficulties are improved:

  • Data entries are consistent.
  • Business users' efficiency is increased by consistency and the proposed values.
  • The most likely value is predicted from the data context.

RAP recommendations enrich value helps with context-aware suggestions. Currently, there are two approaches to implement RAP recommendations: generative AI and deterministic rules.

Implementation

Before implementing RAP recommendations in your application, we need to look at the prerequisites and steps.

Prerequisite

RAP recommendations are built on top of a value help. Therefore, you need to have a value help implemented for the same field where you want to implement the RAP recommendation.

Steps

  1. Define RAP recommendations in the behavior definition.
  2. Implement RAP recommendations in the behavior pool of your RAP business object (using generative AI or deterministic rules).
  3. Define RAP recommendations in the projection behavior definition.

To understand RAP recommendations in detail, you can download the ABAP Flight Reference Scenario (GitHub) for RAP recommendations on GitHub. There, RAP recommendations are used in a travel example: After typing a description, generative AI suggests a destination, and then another RAP recommendation is triggered, suggesting accommodations using deterministic rules. Below is this example in a shorter form.

Example

This example shows the implementation of RAP recommendations using generative AI and deterministic rules. As mentioned above, you can download this example on GitHub. The following screenshot shows the RAP application requesting travel information.

RAP Recommendations

In the example, the business user selected Bulgaria as the destination. Right after the selection, three (fictive) accommodation recommendations for Sofia appear, and the business user can select the most fitting entry. Now, we will get through the implementation details step by step.

Behavior Definition

Define the field for which you want to provide RAP recommendations:

field ( recommendations ) Destination, Accommodation;

Here, the two fields Destination and Accommodation are specified to show the recommended values on the UI.

Define the function that runs the behavior:

recommendation function GetRecommendedValuesFunction;

Hint: Only one function can be specified for the recommended values.

Define side effects to specify which field change triggers the recommendation function:

side effects
  {
    recommendation function GetRecommendedValuesFunction
      executed on field Description, field Destination;
  }

The input for the recommended values is based on the two fields Description and Destination.

Behavior Pool

Define how the recommended values are returned:

METHOD GetRecommendedValuesFunction.
    READ ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_traveltp_rec IN LOCAL MODE
       ENTITY Travel
       FIELDS ( Description Destination ) WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
       RESULT DATA(business_user_travel)
       FAILED failed.

    "...

    LOOP AT business_user_travel ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<travel>).
      "...

      INSERT VALUE #(  %tky = <travel>-%tky
                       %param-travel = VALUE #( destination = CORRESPONDING #( destination_rec_values )
                                                accommodation = CORRESPONDING #( accommodation_rec_values ) ) ) INTO TABLE result.
    ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

The fields Description and Destination of the Travel entity are read and returned in the business_user_travel internal table. Inside the LOOP statement, the transactional key %tky and the result parameter %param for the component travel are filled.

You can find a description of the complete example in Example: Implementing RAP Recommendations (SAP Help Portal).

Deterministic Rules

For the deterministic rules approach, you can, for example, simply implement a query with a condition against your business user data. For example:

SELECT DISTINCT acc_name
          FROM /dmo/acc_rec_h AS accommodation
          INNER JOIN _user_travel AS destination
          ON accommodation~acc_country = destination~Destination
        INTO TABLE @result.

In this query, the Destination value is compared with the generated acc_country value.

Generative AI

To use the generative AI approach, you need to set up a scenario using the ABAP AI SDK (SAP Help Portal). In your Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) scenario, you can define prompt settings that are used to call the LLM Completion API. When the call is successful, the LLM's answer is returned. For example:

METHOD get_completion_ai.
    DATA(completion_api_instance) = get_completion_api_instance( ).

    IF completion_api_instance IS BOUND.
      TRY.
          DATA(completion_api_result) = completion_api_instance->execute_for_string( prompt_setting ).
        CATCH cx_aic_completion_api.
          RETURN result.
      ENDTRY.

      result = completion_api_result->get_completion( ).
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD get_completion_api_instance.
    TRY.
        result = cl_aic_islm_compl_api_factory=>get( )->create_instance( 'MyIntelligentScen' ).
      CATCH cx_aic_api_factory.
        RETURN result.
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.

You can find a description of the complete example in Example: Implementing Generative AI Features for RAP Recommendations (SAP Help Portal).

Recommended Values

The recommended values expect a certain set of parameters:

INSERT VALUE #( value = <rec_values>
                score = 100 - counter
                suggested = COND #( WHEN counter = 1
                                    THEN abap_true
                                    ELSE abap_false ) ) INTO TABLE result.

value is a mandatory parameter and contains the recommended value. score and suggested are optional parameters. Using score, you can order the recommended values in the value help. Using suggested, you can enter a recommended value directly into the input field.

Projection Behavior Definition

To reuse RAP recommendations in the projection behavior definition, you need to add the following:

use recommendations;
use side effects;

Resources

  • Documentation on the overall concept (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Help Portal)
  • Documentation on the implementation (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Help Portal)
  • Syntax Documentation (ABAP Keyword Documentation | SAP Help Portal)

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2605
entry.content:
SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2605

On May 16, the new Release 2605 was delivered. This blog post lists all delivered features and provides an overview. Highlights of the release include new ABAP Language features, the Manage News app for MyHome, the Review Booklets for embedded analytics, and the new Manage SAP System Upgrade app.

The list of all new features including more information can be found on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP environment page. The new AI features are listed separately on the What's New for ABAP Generative AI in ABAP Cloud. The first delivery of ADT for VS Code is independent from the ABAP Backend release and will be rolled-out separately. 

ABAP Language

  • Support of boolean expressions
  • Recursive references as enhancement of the ABAP Type System 
  • ABAP statement DELETE DUPLICATES without sorting

ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • Signature comparison during CDS Migration from DDIC-based views to CDS view entities (see Signature Comparison and Migrating CDS DDIC-based Views to CDS View Entities)
  • Using bracket highlighting for ABAP CDS objects
  • WYSIWYG editor support for Review Booklet pages with two data providers (details)

ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)

  • Support of RAP Feature Control attributes readonly:create and mandatory:update (details)
  • Support of CDS Table Entities as draft persistence in RAP (details)

Analytics

  • Flexible Content Types for Review Booklets with Multiple Data Providers (details)
  • Enhanced App State Handling for Review Booklets (details)

Reuse Services

  • XCO Generation API for CDS Table Entities (details)
  • Maintain Translations app for the translation of email templates (details)

Business Configuration

  • You can now select Tree Table as table type in the SMBC object (details)

ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC)

  • Integration of ATC system in SAP BTP with ChaRM (details)
  • Improved ATC Check Results and Quick Fixes for SAP S/4HANA Custom Code Analysis 
  • New ATC Check Variant for Clean Core ABAP_CLEAN_CORE_READINESS to perform Clean Core analysis on SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA systems

Custom Code Analysis

  • Update accessible resources in Cloud Connector (see SAP Note 2861842 and SAP Note 3548772)
  • Manage Number of ATC Processes via Elastic Scaling (details)

SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • New app Manage News to author and manage news content that appears on the My Home page (details)
  • Reset Single Parameters in the Manage Launchpad Settings App (details)

Landscape Portal

  • Deploy Product: Install multiple add-ons in the same system (details)
  • New app Manage SAP System Upgrade (details)

Infrastructure

  • Transparent start time of the technical downtime for the next quartely upgrade (details)
  • Reduced minimum ABAP Runtime size with 0.5 ACU (details)
  • New Multi-Availability Zone Option with SAP HANA Cloud database configuration in a high-availability setup with a primary and a secondary instance in different availability zones (details)
  • Two new regions on Microsoft Azure: Toronto / Canada and Frankfurt / Germany (details)

entry.title: Exploring ADT for Visual Studio Code and the Agentic Developer Experience
entry.content:
Join our next SAP BTP ABAP Environment Customer & Partner Roundtable on 02.06.2026 (10:00–11:00 CET).

This session includes:

🔹Product Updates (Frank Jentsch)

🔹ADT for Visual Studio Code with Agentic Developer Experience (Thomas Alexander Ritter)

The roundtable is a monthly series taking place on the first Tuesday of every month (10:00–11:00 CET).

👉 Download the .ics calendar file for the full 2026 series and import it directly into your calendar.
Each calendar entry already includes the Microsoft Teams meeting link.

Microsoft Teams meeting link (valid for the entire 2026 series): Microsoft Teams meeting link

SAP Community – SAP BTP ABAP Environment Customer & Partner Roundtable.

If you’re interested in presenting in one of our upcoming sessions, feel free to reach out.

entry.title: How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Introduction

If you've built a custom Fiori application on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) and want to expose it as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone, this tutorial walks you through the complete process — from deployment in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to the final working tile in Work Zone.
This guide assumes you already have:
  • A working RAP-based OData service on S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • A Fiori application project in BAS that consumes this service
  • Access to SAP Build Work Zone (standard edition)
  • The app accessible via ADT preview

By the end of this tutorial, your app will be accessible as a tile in your Work Zone site, sitting alongside other tiles like My Inbox.

Architecture Overview

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)
        │
        │  Deploy to ABAP Repository
        ▼
SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 
   ├── OData Service (Service Binding)
   ├── IAM App (External App) + LADI
   ├── Business Catalog
   └── Business Role
        │
        │  Semantic Object + Action
        ▼
SAP Build Work Zone (BTP)
   └── Tile → resolves intent → opens S/4 app via SSO

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system with developer access
  • SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with a destination configured to your S/4 system
  • SAP Build Work Zone subscription on your BTP subaccount
  • A working Fiori app project in BAS (currently accessible via ADT preview only)
  • Both BTP and S/4 trusting the same SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) tenant for SSO

Step 1: Configure crossNavigation in manifest.json

Before deploying, your Fiori app needs to declare how it will be launched from the Fiori Launchpad. This is done via the crossNavigation/inbounds section in manifest.json.
Open webapp/manifest.json and add the following inside the "sap.app" section:
"crossNavigation": {
  "inbounds": {
    "intent1": {
      "semanticObject": "ZObjectName",
      "action": "display",
      "signature": {
        "parameters": {},
        "additionalParameters": "allowed"
      },
      "title": "App Title",
      "subTitle": "SAP Fiori application",
      "icon": "sap-icon://icon_name"
    }
  }
}
Key points:
  • The semanticObject and action together form the intent (#ZObjectName-display) that the Fiori Launchpad and Work Zone will use to navigate to your app.
  • Choose a meaningful semantic object name (prefix with Z for custom).
  • The title here will appear on the tile.
  • To browse available icons for your tile, see the Icon Explorer . 
Important: Also ensure your i18n property in sap.app uses the simple string format:
"i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
Do NOT use the object format: { "bundleName": "..." }

Note: The S/4HANA Cloud LADI converter cannot process the object format for i18n. Use the simple string format instead. For more details on this issue, see:
UI5 CLI - GitHub Issue #432 
SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help 

Your manifest.json should look like: 

 

Step 2: Ensure i18n.properties File Exists

Make sure the file webapp/i18n/i18n.properties exists and contains at minimum:
appTitle=App Title
appDescription=SAP Fiori Application
Without this file, the LADI creation will fail with the error: "File is not a 'properties' file for texts."

Step 3: Configure Deployment to ABAP

In BAS, configure the deployment target. There are two ways to do this:
Option A: Via Application Info (UI-based)
  1. Right-click your project and select "Open Application Info"
  2. Click the "Deploy" option
  3. Fill in the deployment configuration fields
Option B: Via terminal command
npm run deploy-config
This launches the same configuration wizard in the terminal.
 
Both approaches will prompt you to fill in the following fields:

FieldValue
Target TypeABAP
DestinationYour S/4 destination 
SAPUI5 ABAP RepositoryA unique name, max 15 chars (eg: ZREPO_NAME)
Deployment DescriptionAny suitable description 
PackageYour ABAP package
Transport RequestYour transport request (or leave empty for $TMP)

This generates a ui5-deploy.yaml file:

Step 4: Deploy the Application

Run the deployment from the BAS terminal:
npm run deploy
This builds the app and deploys it to the S/4HANA Cloud ABAP repository.

What to look for in the output:

SAPUI5 Application has been uploaded and registered successfully
Updated LADIs for UI5 Repository "", package "", transport ""
Inbound intent1 (ZObjectName-display) was converted to LADI ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
Launchpad App Descriptor Item ZREPO_NAME_UI5R was updated
Deployment Successful.

For example:

 

If you see warnings like:
  • "No LADI was created as no inbound is defined in manifest.json" → You're missing the crossNavigation block
  • "Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s) / File is not a 'properties' file for texts" → Change i18n to the simple string format

Step 5: Create an IAM App (External App) in S/4

After deployment, the LADI exists on the system but isn't yet linked to any access control. You need to create a new IAM App to hold it and grant access to the OData service.
  1. Open your S/4HANA Cloud system
  2. Search for and open the "Create IAM App" application
  3. Create a new IAM App with:
    • App Type: External App
    • IAM App ID: A meaningful name 
  4. In the "Services" section:
    • Add your OData service
    • This grants the app authorization to call the backend service
  5. In the "Fiori Launchpad App Descr Items" section:
    • Add the LADI ID: ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
    • This enables Launchpad intent navigation
  6. Save and publish

 

 

Why a separate IAM App?
Your OData service already has its own IAM App (type: Custom Inbound Service App) for API access. The External App IAM App is specifically for Launchpad navigation.

Step 6: Add IAM App to a Business Catalog

  • Open Maintain Business Catalogs (or create a new catalog in ADT)
  • Open or create your catalog 
  • Add your new External App IAM App to this catalog
  • Publish the catalog

 

 

Step 7: Assign Business Catalog to a Business Role

  • Open Maintain Business Roles
  • Open the Business Role assigned to your users
  • Go to Assigned Business Catalogs
  • Add your catalog 
  • Save

Step 8: Verify on S/4 Fiori Launchpad

Before adding the tile in Work Zone, verify the app works natively on S/4:
https://<your-system>.lab.s4hana.cloud.sap/ui#ZObjectName-display
Your app should open without errors.
If you get "navigation target could not be resolved", double-check:
  • The LADI is added to the IAM App
  • The IAM App is in a Business Catalog
  • The Business Catalog is in your user's Business Role
  • You've logged out and back in after making changes

Step 9: Add the Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Now that the app works on S/4 via intent navigation, add it to Work Zone:
  • Open SAP Build Work Zone administration
  • Go to Content Manager
  • Create a new App
In the Navigation tab:
  • Semantic Object: ZObjectName (same as the one in manifest.json)
  • Action: display
In the Visualization tab:
  • Title, subtitle, icon as desired
  • Save the app

Step 10: Assign to Role and Group

  • In Content Manager → Roles: Open your role and assign the new app
  • In Content Manager → Groups: Open your group (e.g., "SBPA") and add the app
  • Ensure the role is mapped to a Role Collection that your users have

Step 11: Verify the Tile in Work Zone

Open your Work Zone site.
Your new tile should appear in the designated group/section.
Click it — the app opens seamlessly via SSO (since both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant).

Troubleshooting

Issue CauseFix
"No LADI was created" during deployMissing crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.jsonAdd the crossNavigation block and redeploy
"Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s)"i18n uses object formatChange to "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
"Navigation target could not be resolved"LADI not linked to IAM App, or missing catalog/role assignmentAdd LADI to External App IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role
403 Forbidden / UCON blockedTrying to access direct BSP URLUse Launchpad URL (/ui#SemanticObject-action)
Login prompt (username/password) in Work ZoneURL uses -api endpoint, or SSO not configuredUse non-api URL; ensure both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant
"Destination https://https://..." errorDouble protocol in URLRemove the extra https:// prefix

Key Takeaways

  • crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.json is what creates the LADI during deployment.
  • i18n must use the simple string format ("i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties") for the LADI converter to work.
  • A separate IAM App of type External App is needed for the Launchpad tile.
  • The chain must be complete: LADI → IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role → User
  • In Work Zone, use semantic object + action — not URL tiles.
  • SSO works automatically when both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant.

Conclusion

The path from "app works in ADT preview" to "tile in Work Zone" requires a clear understanding of the deployment chain:
  • BAS deploys the UI and creates the LADI
  • The IAM App links the LADI to access control
  • Work Zone resolves the semantic intent
Each piece is simple on its own, but missing any single step results in confusing errors.
Once this flow is established, adding future apps follows the same pattern — making it repeatable and scalable across your organization.

Tags

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Business Application Studio, Fiori, RAP, ABAP Cloud, LADI, IAM App, Tile Configuration

entry.title: Sapphire 2026 recap: Joule for Developers Agentic ABAP AI
entry.content:
TL;DR: What you learned on Sapphire 2026 is that in general SAP is evolving from standalone AI tools to an orchestration layer that automates multi-step workflows for business user and developers.
The ABAP platform team is very proud to be on the forefront of this and this was the major focus for all Sapphire ABAP AI sessions. 
In a nutshell we have announced the following key benefits for customers and partners of what will be made generally available in the first weeks of June:

 

  1. ABAP AI now supports Agentic orchestration in Eclipse and VS Code with out-of-the-box ABAP MCP Server lots of great MCP tools.
  2. ABAP AI is available for lower releases of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition >=2021.
  3. Simplified enablement of ABAP AI via SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration with a new commercial model based on AI Unit consumption (->details how to purchase and on-board / AI Units overview).
  4. Faster innovation with Side-by-Side consumption of SAP and 3rd party Agentic IDE, Orchestrators and LLMs (e.g. Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral…)
  5. Agentic Custom Code Migration accelerates ECC to S/4HANA Transformation -> see the demo below

 

Here is a demo of the first iteration of the agentic custom code migration with mass ready ATC AI quick fixes using Amazon Q in Eclipse (on Sapphire we showed also GitHub CoPilot as orchestrator):

If you want to understand deeper the context of the demo have a look into my Sapphire recap. Here I explain in more detail what it means for you as customer and developer and I also give a bit of outlook to what comes next:

 

More details can be found here:

  1. Sapphire 2026: GA announcement for agentic ABAP AI (Sonja Lienard)
  2. Sapphire 2026: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic! (Alexander Rother)
  3. How to purchase Agentic ABAP AI
  4. AI Units overview
  5. VS Code: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code is now available on the VS Code marketplace! 
  6. FAQ document for frequent questions
  7. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  8. Help & Guides
    1. Free Promo only (not yet AI Units): Step-by-step guide how to activate free promo for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    2. Free Promo only (not yet AI Units): Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: Develop RAP-Based SAP Fiori Applications in Minutes with SAP Fiori Tools AI Project Accelerator
entry.content:
Last year, as described in the blog From idea to app: using AI-powered Project Accelerator to develop SAP Fiori apps, the Project Accelerator focused on generating CAP-based projects,

Developing enterprise SAP Fiori applications using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a standardized way to build modern cloud-ready applications. These applications are typically developed in environments such as SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (version 2602 or higher).

In a significant step forward, the Project Accelerator now supports RAP-based projects In this post, I will explain why the addition of RAP is important and then describe the specific steps  to build an app using this new capability.

The addition of RAP support makes the AI Project Accelerator more relevant for enterprise SAP development by aligning it with SAP’s strategic ABAP model. It enables end-to-end generation of backend artifacts and frontend fully functional SAP Fiori elements application including manifest and annotation configurations. Furthermore, it supports the Clean Core 4-Level Extension Model, as the generated backend artifacts fall under Level A—the cleanest form of extensions.

The AI-powered Project Accelerator in SAP Fiori tools is available in SAP Business Application Studio (SBAS) under a SAP Build Code subscription ( is available).

Application ideas or business requirements can be provided by developers, designers, and business experts in various forms, such as:

  1. Text
  2. Image or UI mock-up (.jpg, .png)
  3. Combination of text and image as a business requirement document (.docx, .md)

The project accelerator enables rapid prototyping of a fully functional application that can be showcased and then extended using SAP Fiori tools or any coding assistants (such as Cline, Claude, and Copilot) integrated with MCP servers (for example, MCP server for SAP Fiori or CAP) for further development.

It generates the complete application stack from scratch, including the CDS data model, behavior definitions, service definitions, OData V4 service, and an SAP Fiori elements application. This follows a greenfield approach, with no reuse of existing business objects.

Getting Started

Now that we understand the AI Project Accelerator, let’s proceed with the step-by-step walkthrough to generate the RAP project using an SAP BTP trial account.

Before we begin, complete the following prerequisites:

  1. Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial (follow step 1 to step 5 and in step 6, choose region US East- AWS) . Note: The trial version of SAP Build Code is currently only available in the US East - AWS region.
  2. Start Booster- Prepare an Account for ABAP Trial (only step 1)
  3. Start Booster – Get started with SAP Build Code

Navigate to subaccount(trial) and verify Instances and subscriptions.

Create a Destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Manually add below additional properties to destination and continue to review step.

  • WebIDEEnabled: true
  • abap_enabled: true
  • WebIDEUsage: odata_abap,dev_abap,abap_cloud
  • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  • HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false
  • HTML5.Timeout: 360000
Verify all the details of destination and click on create.    
 

Configure ABAP Development Tools (ADT) and create new ABAP package.

  1. You have installed ABAP Development Tools, latest version
  2. Create new ABAP Cloud Project.
  3. Copy URL from destination properties.
  4. Create new ABAP package Z_<Unique Name> under super package ZLOCAL

SAP Build Code - Create new dev space

  1. Open the SAP Build Code lobby.
 
 
 
Create new Dev space that contains SAP Fiori tools extensions in SAP Business Application Studio.
 
 
Provide a Dev space name (for example: FioriAppDevelopment).
 

Once the Dev space is created and starts running, click on FioriAppDevelopment to open SAP Business Application Studio

Launching the SAP Fiori Tools AI: Project Accelerator

 Go to Project Explorer view and Click on Open Folder, to open your workspace folder (/home/user/projects).

 
Open the Command Palette

Search for “Fiori application” and select “Fiori: Open Application Generator”.

 
For AI-assisted SAP Fiori app creation click on "Project Accelerator"
 
Select RAP Accelerator.
  • Select the destination.
  • Enter the ABAP repository name
  • Choose the package for back-end artifacts generation
  • Provide a transport request (if required)
  • Not needed for local packages
  • Click Connect to proceed.
 

Generating Your First SAP Fiori Elements Application

 The AI Project Accelerator supports multiple input formats. One powerful approach is generating SAP Fiori application from images.

I have a hand-drawn sketch representing a Manage Purchase Orders application. the expectation from the Project Accelerator is to interpret the visual design and underlying business intent, identify floorplan and generate a RAP based SAP Fiori elements application. The generated application should closely reflect the structure, fields, and layout depicted in the image, converting the sketch into a fully functional List Report and Object Page.

Download manage_purchase_orders.png to your local disk and upload it to the Projects Folder. Drag and drop the image to the projects pane on the left.

Click on Choose File and select the manage_purchase_orders.png

 

You can view the generation logs to understand how SAP Fiori tools AI generates your application and if needed, to gather additional details for troubleshooting. // Add as side-info

Click on Generate. The project accelerator will analyze the image and generate a, including back-end OData V4 service artifacts and front-end artifacts. 

For supported features and known restrictions, refer to the documentation

In ABAP Development Tools (ADT), check the destination system and ensure that all backend OData V4 service artifacts are generated and available in the selected package.

 

. 

 

After the generation process completes, the new project (e.g., ai-created-rap) is added to your workspace, and the Application Information page opens automatically.

On the Application Information page, under Common Tasks, click Preview Application.

  • Select start-mock to preview the application with AI-generated mock data.
  • Alternatively, use the start script to preview the application with the backend; however, the backend will not have any data as it is newly generated.

You will then see the SAP Fiori elements application with a List Report (including a filter bar and a table for purchase orders. Compare the generated SAP Fiori application with the uploaded image to verify how closely the project accelerator has interpreted the floorplan, fields, and structure.

.       

Overall, the AI Project Accelerator for RAP can significantly speed up the development of RAP-based SAP Fiori applications, enabling developers, designers, and business users to transform ideas, requirements, or even sketches into functional enterprise apps within minutes.

References:

Generating an Application with the Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI

Why use the AI-powered Project Accelerator from SAP Fiori tools?

SAP Fiori Tools Update: Elevating Development with AI, Usability, and Flexibility

entry.title: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code is now available on the VS Code marketplace!
entry.content:
Introduction

On behalf of the ABAP development tools team, I am pleased to announce that the first version of the ADT for VS Code extension is available on the VS Code marketplace! Go ahead, install the extension and use it against any ABAP system that supports the ABAP development tools for Eclipse today.

How to install and use the extension?

Simple.

1. Install VS Code: https://code.visualstudio.com/

2. Open the extension page on the VS Code marketplace and click the Install button: extension page

3. Refer to the official getting started guide for guidance on how to set up your ABAP system connection. If you are already using ADT for Eclipse no ABAP system changes are needed. Otherwise, use our admin guide to configure the system for client access.

4. Check out our tutorial on GitHub to learn how to use the new ADT MCP server with an AI agent to build a simple RAP application.

How can I use the built-in MCP server?

The extension comes with a first version of the ADT MCP server. For security reasons, the server is disabled by default. It needs to be enabled via the extension settings by checking the checkbox (see screenshot). Once enabled, the server will start automatically. The documentation how to configure the MCP server can be found here.

Regardless the chat agent solution you use, we recommend defining ABAP related rules via a custom AGENTS.md file. Our documentation provides a template which you can use as a starting point.

What can you expect?

The first version represents roughly 11 months of core development time. The main focus was on designing and implementing the foundational architecture for the extension and the feature scope for RAP UI service development. Developers primarily working on RAP UI services can now develop the frontend and backend parts of their SAP Fiori/SAPUI5 app in the same tool. The extension also includes development support from AI agents such as Microsoft Copilot. This was one of the most requested features from the ABAP developer community.

In short, how useful the extension is for you will depend on your most common ABAP-related development tasks. A list of key features can be found here.

What comes next?

With most of the foundational work done, the focus is now on adding missing features.

Here are some planned highlights for the next release:

  • Support for RFC logon via password
  • Support of more core development object types: RAP behavior extensions, DDIC tables, DDIC structures, DDIC enqueue objects, DDIC type groups, CDS entity buffers, programs/includes, function groups and modules, ...
  • MCP tools: Unified diff tool for transports, ABAP test cockpit tools, ...

Follow the ABAP Cloud Roadmap if you are interested in knowing what comes next.

Provide feedback and influence our product backlog!

We welcome your feedback! Feel free to use the comment section to let us know which features you are missing the most. Your input will help us prioritize the feature backlog for the next releases.

Still some questions left?

Take a look at FAQ document .

Related articles

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/abap-development-tools-for-vs-code-everything-you-need-to-know/ba-p/14258129
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/behind-the-design-how-we-transformed-the-abap-development-tools/ba-p/14258121

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment – How to Correct the System Type
entry.content:
What are System Types?

SAP BTP ABAP Environment (a.k.a. Steampunk) distinguishes two types of systems. The system type is set via parameter is_development_allowed (true or false) during system creation. The default is true (see documentation). Until now, the parameter value cannot be changed for an existing system. The two system types are:

  • development system - parameter is_development_allowed = true
  • non-development system - parameter is_development_allowed = false

The main characteristics are:

CharacteristicsDevelopmentNon-Development
System flavoursdevelopment, sandboxtest, production
Manual changes of development objects are allowed in ADTyesno (objects can only be changed via SWC clone/pull or add-on deployment)
Developer role SAP_BR_DEVELOPER does existyesno
Downtime-optimized update and upgrade appliednoyes

 

Why a Correction Might be Needed?

As the default during system creation is development system, the setting is sometimes not considered as important and not changed properly for test and production system. A typical mistake is, that test and productive systems are setup as development system. An incorrect setup has a significant impact as explained in the table above. Therefore, a correct setup is highly recommended, especially to benefit from the downtime-optimized updates and upgrades (see blog post).

 

How to Correct the System Type?

Prerequistes

Mandatory manual adjustments:

  1. Make sure that there is no open transport.
  2. Make sure that no additional test tenant resides on the system. This system type switch is currently not supported if you have created a test tenant via the Landscape Portal.
  3. Remove all business roles which contain the business catalog SAP_A4C_BC_DEV_PC for development. One of these business roles is the generated role SAP_BR_DEVELOPER. Please remove SAP_BR_DEVELOPER. But there might be also additional custom-defined business roles which need to be adjusted, for example, by removing the business catalog SAP_A4C_BC_DEV_PC from the role definition.

Perform the Correction

For the time being, a service request has to be filed via support ticket on BC-CP-ABA containing the following information:

  • Title: Request for system type switch for SAP BTP ABAP Environment system
  • Description: Please switch <instance id> to a non-development system
  • Priority: Medium

You can find the <instance id> of the respective system either in the SAP BTP Cockpit or in the properties of the respective ABAP Cloud project in the ABAP Development Tools (ADT) as first part of the system URL. Please notice, that the 3-character system id is not necessarily unique in your landscape and therefore not sufficent. 

A downtime of approx. 5 minutes is required for the actual system conversion which will happen during Walldorf business hours.

Validate the Correction

Once the ticket has been processed and your system has been converted, you will find the following inherent changes:

  1. The SAP Fiori Launchpad About dialog show the system role Production System and the tenant role Production Tenant.
  2. The business role template SAP_BR_DEVELOPER is no longer available in the Maintain Business Roles app.

entry.title: Calling an SAP BTP Destination from the SAP BTP ABAP Environment
entry.content:
When building extensions on SAP BTP ABAP Environment, you often need to call external HTTP or OData services.

Instead of hardcoding target URLs in your ABAP code, you can define a destination in the SAP BTP cockpit and consume it from ABAP. This keeps the endpoint configuration outside the code and makes it easier to change later.

In this example, I will call the public Northwind OData service from an ABAP Console Application using a BTP destination.

Create the destination in SAP BTP

In the SAP BTP cockpit, create a destination like this:

Name: Northwind

Type: HTTP

Description: Northwind OData services

Proxy Type: Internet

URL: https://services.odata.org

Authentication: NoAuthentication

After saving the destination, run Check Connection.

For this example, the check was successful:

HTTP request (without authentication) to Northwind destination succeeded.

The service we want to call is:

https://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/Products?$top=5

Because the destination already contains the base URL:

https://services.odata.org

the ABAP code only needs to provide the relative path:

/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/Products?$top=5

Create the ABAP console class

Create a new global class in ADT, for example:

ZCL_NORTHWIND_DESTINATION_TEST

Use this code:

CLASS zcl_northwind_destination_test DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_product,
             productid    TYPE i,
             productname  TYPE string,
             unitprice    TYPE p LENGTH 10 DECIMALS 4,
             unitsinstock TYPE i,
           END OF ty_product.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_response,
             value TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_product WITH EMPTY KEY,
           END OF ty_response.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_northwind_destination_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    TRY.
        DATA(lo_destination) =
          cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_cloud_destination(
            i_name       = 'Northwind'
            i_authn_mode = if_a4c_cp_service=>service_specific
          ).
        DATA(lo_client) =
          cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination(
            lo_destination
          ).
        DATA(lo_request) = lo_client->get_http_request( ).
        lo_request->set_uri_path(
          '/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/Products?$top=5'
        ).
        lo_request->set_header_field(
          i_name  = 'Accept'
          i_value = 'application/json'
        ).
        DATA(lo_response) = lo_client->execute(
          i_method = if_web_http_client=>get
        ).
        DATA(ls_status) = lo_response->get_status( ).
        DATA(lv_body)   = lo_response->get_text( ).
        out->write( |HTTP Status : { ls_status-code } { ls_status-reason }| ).
        DATA(ls_response) = VALUE ty_response( ).
        /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
          EXPORTING
            json        = lv_body
            pretty_name = /ui2/cl_json=>pretty_mode-camel_case
          CHANGING
            data        = ls_response
        ).
        out->write( '===== PRODUCTS =====' ).
        LOOP AT ls_response-value INTO DATA(ls_product).
          out->write(
            |{ ls_product-productid } - { ls_product-productname } | &&
            |Price: { ls_product-unitprice } | &&
            |Stock: { ls_product-unitsinstock }|
          ).
        ENDLOOP.
        lo_client->close( ).
      CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_error).
        out->write( |Error: { lx_error->get_text( ) }| ).
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Run the class using:

Run As > ABAP Application Console

Key part of the code

The destination is consumed using:

DATA(lo_destination) =

  cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_cloud_destination(

    i_name       = 'Northwind'

    i_authn_mode = if_a4c_cp_service=>service_specific

  ).

Then an HTTP client is created from that destination:

DATA(lo_client) =

  cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination(

    lo_destination

  ).

Console output

The output should look like this:

HTTP Status : 200 OK

===== PRODUCTS =====

1 - Chai Price: 18.0000 Stock: 39

2 - Chang Price: 19.0000 Stock: 17

3 - Aniseed Syrup Price: 10.0000 Stock: 13

4 - Chef Anton's Cajun Seasoning Price: 22.0000 Stock: 53

5 - Chef Anton's Gumbo Mix Price: 21.3500 Stock: 0

This confirms that the ABAP class successfully resolved the BTP destination, called the Northwind OData service, received the response, deserialised the JSON, and printed the result.

Note about trial systems

During testing on a trial ABAP environment, the destination-based approach returned:

Destination error: Destination not found

The same destination-based code worked successfully in a non-trial SAP BTP ABAP system.

So if you see this in a trial environment, the issue may be related to destination visibility or Destination service integration in that specific trial setup, not the ABAP code itself.

Conclusion

Calling a BTP destination from SAP BTP ABAP Environment requires only a few lines of code:

This keeps the external system configuration in SAP BTP and allows your ABAP code to stay clean and portable.
